records.ety: [F. forge, fr. L. fabrica the workshop of an artisan who works in hard materials, fr. faber artisan, smith, as adj., skillful, ingenious; cf. Gr. <?/ soft, tender. Cf. Fabric.]

defs: A place or establishment where iron or other metals are wrought by heating and hammering; especially, a furnace, or a shop with its furnace, etc., where iron is heated and wrought; a smithy.
quotes: In the quick forge and working house of thought.

defs: The works where wrought iron is produced directly from the ore, or where iron is rendered malleable by puddling and shingling; a shingling mill.
The act of beating or working iron or steel; the manufacture of metallic bodies.
quotes: In the greater bodies the forge was easy.

records.ety: [F. forger, OF. forgier, fr. L. fabricare, fabricari, to form, frame, fashion, from fabrica. See Forge, n., and cf. Fabricate.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Forged (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Forging (?).]

defs: To form by heating and hammering; to beat into any particular shape, as a metal.
quotes: Mars's armor forged for proof eterne.

defs: To form or shape out in any way; to produce; to frame; to invent.
quotes: Those names that the schools forged, and put into the mouth of scholars, could never get admittance into common use.
Do forge a life-long trouble for ourselves.

defs: To make falsely; to produce, as that which is untrue or not genuine; to fabricate; to counterfeit, as, a signature, or a signed document.
quotes: That paltry story is untrue, And forged to cheat such gulls as you.
Forged certificates of his . . . moral character.

records.ety: [See Forge, v. t., and for sense 2, cf. Forge compel.]

defs: To move heavily and slowly, as a ship after the sails are furled; to work one's way, as one ship in outsailing another; -- used especially in the phrase to forge ahead.
quotes: And off she [a ship] forged without a shock.

defs: To impel forward slowly; as, to forge a ship forward.
